questions.text: Identify the sentence containing an intransitive verb:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: In this sentence, "spoke" is a transitive verb. "The truth" is the direct object of the verb "spoke." Transitive verbs require a direct object to complete their meaning.
In this sentence, "sleeps" is an intransitive verb as it does not require a direct object to complete its meaning.
In this sentence, "ring" is a transitive verb. "The bell" is the direct object of the verb "ring." Transitive verbs require a direct object to complete their meaning.
In this sentence, "fought" is a transitive verb. "The wasps" is the direct object of the verb "fought." Transitive verbs require a direct object to complete their meaning.

questions.explanation: In this sentence, "sleeps" is an intransitive verb as it does not require a direct object to complete its meaning.

questions.text: The story jumped off the page. Which poetic se device has been used in this line?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Metaphor: A metaphor is a figure of speech that makes a comparison between two unrelated things by stating that one thing is another thing. For example, "Time is a thief." In the given sentence, "The story jumped off the page," there is no direct comparison being made between the story and another entity. Therefore, metaphor is not the correct choice.

Paradox: A paradox is a statement that seems self-contradictory or absurd but, in reality, expresses a possible truth. An example would be, "Less is more." The given sentence, "The story jumped off the page," does not contain any inherent contradiction or express a paradoxical idea. Thus, paradox is not the correct choice.

Oxymoron: An oxymoron is a figure of speech that combines contradictory terms. Common examples include "jumbo shrimp" or "bitter-sweet." The given sentence, "The story jumped off the page," does not combine contradictory terms. Instead, it presents a vivid and imaginative image of the story's impact. Therefore, oxymoron is not the correct choice.

The poetic device used in the line "The story jumped off the page" is d) Personification. Personification is the attribution of human qualities or actions to non-human entities, and in this case, the story is being described as if it were a living being capable of jumping off the page.

questions.explanation: The poetic device used in the line "The story jumped off the page" is d) Personification. Personification is the attribution of human qualities or actions to non-human entities, and in this case, the story is being described as if it were a living being capable of jumping off the page.

questions.text: He shaves twenty times a day but still has a beard.The appropriate inference for this sentencwe is:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option doesn't fit the context of the sentence. Shaving twenty times a day doesn't imply laziness, as it actually suggests the opposite—significant effort is being put into shaving.

This option seems to be the most appropriate inference. Shaving twenty times a day is an unusual frequency for most people but aligns with the work of a barber, who would constantly be shaving or grooming others.

options.text: He does not know how to shave
options.explanation: This option doesn't seem likely because someone who shaves twenty times a day likely knows how to shave. The frequency suggests that there might be other reasons for not achieving a clean shave, such as rapid beard growth.

This option doesn't seem relevant to the sentence. The sentence is about shaving frequency and the presence of a beard despite frequent shaving, not about the condition of the equipment used for shaving.

questions.explanation: This option seems to be the most appropriate inference. Shaving twenty times a day is an unusual frequency for most people but aligns with the work of a barber, who would constantly be shaving or grooming others.

questions.text: The judge remained impartial and did not give any undue favor to any party. The underlined word can be best described as:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: To remain unbiased: This option fits the context of the sentence. "Impartial" means to remain unbiased or neutral, which aligns with the behavior of the judge in the given sentence.
To be angry: This option doesn't fit the context of the sentence. "Impartial" doesn't relate to being angry but rather to being fair and unbiased.
To be arrogant: This option doesn't fit the context either. "Impartial" doesn't relate to being arrogant but rather to being fair and unbiased.

To remain calm: While "impartial" might coincide with remaining calm in some situations, it primarily refers to being fair and unbiased rather than being calm. This option isn't as accurate as option a) "To remain unbiased."

questions.explanation: To remain unbiased: This option fits the context of the sentence. "Impartial" means to remain unbiased or neutral, which aligns with the behavior of the judge in the given sentence.

questions.text: As a result, you should be a happy person.The sentence contains a transitional device of:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Consequence: This option is correct. "As a result" indicates that something has happened as a consequence of a previous action or event.
Time: This option is incorrect. "As a result" does not indicate a specific point in time but rather a consequence of something else happening.
Addition: This option is incorrect. "As a result" does not signify adding something but rather the consequence or outcome of a previous action or event.
Comparison: This option is incorrect. "As a result" does not imply comparing anything but rather indicates a cause-and-effect relationship.

questions.explanation: Consequence: This option is correct. "As a result" indicates that something has happened as a consequence of a previous action or event.

questions.text: He said, "Where are you going "?The correct indirect speech of the givensentence is:
questions.subject: English

options.text: He enquired where were we going.
options.explanation: "He enquired where were we going." This option is incorrect because it does not maintain the correct order of the reported speech. In indirect speech, the tense of the reporting verb (in this case, "said") is usually shifted back one tense, and the word order is changed. Therefore, "where were we going" should be changed to "where we were going."

options.text: He enquired where we were going
options.explanation: Option b) "He enquired where we were going" accurately maintains the correct word order, tense, and meaning of the original sentence in indirect speech.

options.text: He suggested as if we were going
options.explanation: "He suggested as if we were going." This option does not accurately convey the content of the original sentence. It changes the meaning from a direct question to a suggestion and does not maintain the original structure.

options.text: He told where were we going
options.explanation: "He told where were we going." This option is incorrect because it does not maintain the correct word order. In indirect speech, the word order should be changed, and the conjunction "that" or "if" should be used to introduce the reported speech. Additionally, the tense of the verb "were" should match the tense of the original sentence. Therefore, "where were we going" should be changed to "where we were going."

questions.explanation: "He enquired where were we going." This option is incorrect because it does not maintain the correct order of the reported speech. In indirect speech, the tense of the reporting verb (in this case, "said") is usually shifted back one tense, and the word order is changed. Therefore, "where were we going" should be changed to "where we were going."

questions.text: _is a genre of English literature that revolves around discovering secrets or solving crimes:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Folktale: Folktale is a genre of literature that includes traditional stories passed down orally from one generation to another. While folktales may contain elements of mystery, they are not specifically focused on discovering secrets or solving crimes.

Fable: Fable is a genre of literature that typically features animals as characters and conveys a moral lesson or teaches a lesson about human behavior. Fables do not necessarily revolve around discovering secrets or solving crimes.
Option c) Mystery accurately describes a genre of English literature that revolves around discovering secrets or solving crimes, often through the use of suspense, clues, and investigation.

Myth: Myth is a genre of literature that often explains the origins of the world, natural phenomena, or cultural traditions through the use of gods, supernatural beings, and legendary figures. Myths are not typically focused on discovering secrets or solving crimes.

questions.explanation: Option c) Mystery accurately describes a genre of English literature that revolves around discovering secrets or solving crimes, often through the use of suspense, clues, and investigation.

questions.text: We sat silently when one of us ended the formality and started talking. The most appropriate idiom to replace the underlined part of the sentence is:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Bear the brunt: This idiom means to endure the worst part of something difficult or unpleasant. It doesn't fit the context of the sentence, which describes breaking the silence and initiating conversation.
Blow hot and cold: This idiom means to be inconsistent or indecisive in one's actions or attitudes. It doesn't convey the idea of initiating conversation after a period of silence.

Option c) Break the ice is the most appropriate idiom because it means to initiate a conversation or social interaction in a situation where there is tension or silence, which aligns with the context of the sentence.

Bring down the house: This idiom means to elicit enthusiastic applause or a strong positive reaction from an audience, often in the context of a performance or event. It doesn't fit the context of the sentence, which describes initiating conversation.

questions.explanation: Option c) Break the ice is the most appropriate idiom because it means to initiate a conversation or social interaction in a situation where there is tension or silence, which aligns with the context of the sentence.

questions.text: He had been reading a book since morning. Which tense is this?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Present Indefinite: This tense would be "He reads a book since morning." However, "since morning" suggests an action that started in the past and continues into the present, so the present indefinite tense is not appropriate here.

Past Indefinite: This tense would be "He read a book since morning." However, "since morning" indicates a time period that began in the past and continues into the present, so the past indefinite tense doesn't convey the ongoing nature of the action.
The tense used in the sentence "He had been reading a book since morning" is:c) Present Perfect Continuous

Past Perfect Continuous: This tense would be "He had been reading a book since morning." However, "since morning" implies that the action started in the past and continues into the present, so there's no need to use the past perfect tense to indicate a completed action before another past action. Also, the past perfect continuous tense implies an action that was ongoing for a period of time in the past and was completed before another past action, which doesn't fit the context of the sentence.

questions.explanation: The tense used in the sentence "He had been reading a book since morning" is:c) Present Perfect Continuous

questions.text: The road was a ribbon of moonlight. Which poetic device has been used in this line?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Metaphor: A metaphor creates a direct comparison between two things without using "like" or "as." In this sentence, the road is directly compared to a ribbon of moonlight, highlighting its pale, shimmering quality.
Paradox: A paradox is a statement that seems contradictory but may contain a layer of truth. This sentence doesn't present any kind of contradiction.
Oxymoron: An oxymoron combines two opposite ideas into a single phrase (e.g. deafening silence). This sentence doesn't use contrasting terms.
Simile: A simile uses "like" or "as" to compare two things. This sentence doesn't use either of those words.

questions.explanation: Metaphor: A metaphor creates a direct comparison between two things without using "like" or "as." In this sentence, the road is directly compared to a ribbon of moonlight, highlighting its pale, shimmering quality.

questions.text: Which one of the following sentences contains an opinion?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The cake tastes delicious: This sentence expresses a personal preference for the taste of the cake. Deliciousness is subjective and depends on individual taste.

options.text: The heart pumps blood to the body.
options.explanation: The heart pumps blood to the body: This is a scientific fact that can be objectively verified.

options.text: The sun sets in the West.
options.explanation: The sun sets in the West: This is a statement based on our observation of the earth's rotation. It's a verifiable fact

options.text: The skin is the body's largest organ.
options.explanation: The skin is the body's largest organ: This is another scientific fact about human anatomy.

questions.explanation: The cake tastes delicious: This sentence expresses a personal preference for the taste of the cake. Deliciousness is subjective and depends on individual taste.

questions.text: My favorite teacher, Mr. Jack teaches English. The underlined part is:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Appositive phrase: An appositive phrase renames or clarifies the noun or noun phrase before it. In this case, "Mr. Jack" renames the noun phrase "My favorite teacher".
Infinitive phrase: An infinitive phrase uses the infinitive form of a verb (to + verb) and functions as a noun, adjective, or adverb. This is not the case here.
Noun phrase: A noun phrase includes a noun and its modifiers (articles, adjectives). "My favorite teacher" is a noun phrase, but the underlined part narrows it down further.
Verb phrase: A verb phrase includes a verb and its helping verbs (tense, mood). This sentence doesn't have a verb phrase underlined.

questions.explanation: Appositive phrase: An appositive phrase renames or clarifies the noun or noun phrase before it. In this case, "Mr. Jack" renames the noun phrase "My favorite teacher".

questions.text: The stars were bright, and we could see our way. The sentence is a_ sentence.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Simple sentence: A simple sentence has one independent clause (which can stand alone as a complete thought). This sentence is not simple because it has two independent clauses.

Compound sentence: A compound sentence has two or more independent clauses joined by a coordinating conjunction (for, and, nor, but, or, so, yet). This sentence is complex because one clause depends on the other.

Complex sentence: A complex sentence has an independent clause and a dependent clause (which cannot stand alone as a complete thought). "The stars were bright" is an independent clause, but "and we could see our way" depends on the first part for complete meaning.
Compound-complex sentence: A compound-complex sentence has two or more independent clauses and at least one dependent clause. This sentence does not have multiple independent clauses.

questions.explanation: Complex sentence: A complex sentence has an independent clause and a dependent clause (which cannot stand alone as a complete thought). "The stars were bright" is an independent clause, but "and we could see our way" depends on the first part for complete meaning.

questions.text: Which one of the following sentences exemplifies the use of lrony?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A fire station burnt down: This is an unfortunate event, but it doesn't involve a contrast in meaning.

options.text: I must be cruel inly to be kind
options.explanation: Irony: Irony involves a contrast between appearance and reality, expectation and outcome, or what is said and what is meant. In this sentence, "cruel" and "kind" are opposite ideas. Being cruel to be kind suggests a situation where a difficult or unpleasant action is necessary for a good outcome, even though it seems contradictory.

options.text: It is a hot and humid today.
options.explanation: It is a hot and humid day: This is a simple observation about the weather.

options.text: Hope for the Best; Prepare for the worst
options.explanation: Hope for the Best; Prepare for the Worst: This proverb expresses wisdom and practical thinking, but it doesn't involve a contradiction.

questions.explanation: Irony: Irony involves a contrast between appearance and reality, expectation and outcome, or what is said and what is meant. In this sentence, "cruel" and "kind" are opposite ideas. Being cruel to be kind suggests a situation where a difficult or unpleasant action is necessary for a good outcome, even though it seems contradictory.

questions.text: If you want to succeed, beware _ the black sheep among you.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: About: "About" often suggests a general topic of discussion. In this context, it wouldn't be as strong a warning.
Of: "Of" indicates a specific concern. "Beware of the black sheep" clearly warns against something particular within the group.
By: "By" doesn't typically fit in this context.
Under: "Under" suggests being beneath something, which isn't the intended meaning here.

questions.explanation: Of: "Of" indicates a specific concern. "Beware of the black sheep" clearly warns against something particular within the group.

questions.text: You would have gotten wet if it had rained. The sentence is an example of:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Type Zero conditional: These sentences express general truths or habits. They use the present tense in both clauses (e.g., "If you heat water, it boils"). This sentence doesn't express a general truth.

Type I probable conditional: These sentences talk about possible future situations. They use the present tense in the "if" clause and "will" in the main clause (e.g., "If you study hard, you will pass the exam"). This sentence talks about a hypothetical situation in the past.

Type II improbable conditional: These sentences discuss less probable future situations. They use the past tense in the "if" clause and "would" in the main clause (e.g., "If I won the lottery, I would travel the world"). This sentence talks about a situation in the past that didn't happen.

Type III unfulfilled conditional: These sentences discuss hypothetical situations in the past that did not happen. They use the past perfect tense in the "if" clause and "would have" + past participle in the main clause (e.g., "If I had studied harder, I would have passed the exam"). This is exactly the structure used in the given sentence.

questions.explanation: Type III unfulfilled conditional: These sentences discuss hypothetical situations in the past that did not happen. They use the past perfect tense in the "if" clause and "would have" + past participle in the main clause (e.g., "If I had studied harder, I would have passed the exam"). This is exactly the structure used in the given sentence.

questions.text: Which one of the following sentences contains an adjective phrase?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "He fell to the ground.": This sentence does not contain an adjective phrase. It describes an action (falling) but does not include a group of words functioning as an adjective.

options.text: Nelson was a boy without fear.
options.explanation: An adjective phrase is a group of words that function together to describe or modify a noun or pronoun in a sentence. In this sentence, the phrase "without fear" acts as an adjective phrase modifying the noun "boy." It describes a characteristic of the boy Nelson.

options.text: Horses prefer living in dark stables.
options.explanation: "Horses prefer living in dark stables.": This sentence includes the phrase "in dark stables," but it functions as an adverbial phrase modifying the verb "prefer," not as an adjective phrase.
"Do not talk like that.": This sentence does not contain an adjective phrase. It gives a command but does not include a group of words functioning as an adjective.

questions.explanation: An adjective phrase is a group of words that function together to describe or modify a noun or pronoun in a sentence. In this sentence, the phrase "without fear" acts as an adjective phrase modifying the noun "boy." It describes a characteristic of the boy Nelson.

questions.text: She saw a smiling girl across the road. The underlined part of the sentence is:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Infinitive: An infinitive is the verb form preceded by "to" (e.g., to smile, to see). This is not the case here.

Present participle: A present participle is a verb form ending in "-ing" that functions as an adjective (modifying a noun or pronoun) or an adverb (modifying a verb, adjective, or another adverb). In this case, "smiling" describes the girl, making it a present participle.
Past participle: A past participle is a verb form that has undergone a change to indicate past tense or completion (e.g., smiled, seen). This is not the case here.
Helping verb: A helping verb helps the main verb form a tense or mood. There is no helping verb in the underlined phrase.

questions.explanation: Present participle: A present participle is a verb form ending in "-ing" that functions as an adjective (modifying a noun or pronoun) or an adverb (modifying a verb, adjective, or another adverb). In this case, "smiling" describes the girl, making it a present participle.

questions.text: _ darkest cloud has a silver lining.Choose the correct option to complete the sentence:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "A" or "an" are indefinite articles used for unspecified things. In this proverb, we're referring to a specific concept (any cloud).
"A" or "an" are indefinite articles used for unspecified things. In this proverb, we're referring to a specific concept (any cloud).
"Every cloud has a silver lining" is a common proverb. Proverbs often use definite articles ("the") to refer to general truths or concepts.
"No article required" wouldn't be appropriate because the proverb refers to a specific concept, not something general.

questions.explanation: "Every cloud has a silver lining" is a common proverb. Proverbs often use definite articles ("the") to refer to general truths or concepts.

questions.text: They rested when evening came. The underlined part of the sentence is:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Adverb clause: An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or another adverb by expressing time, manner, reason, condition, contrast, etc. In this sentence, "when evening came" modifies the verb "rested" by indicating the time of their rest.

Adjective clause: An adjective clause is a dependent clause that modifies a noun or pronoun in the main clause. For example, "when evening came" is not modifying a noun or pronoun in the main clause; instead, it's providing information about the time frame of the action in the main clause.

Noun clause: A noun clause functions as a noun within a sentence. It can act as the subject, object, or complement of the verb. "When evening came" does not function as a noun within the sentence; rather, it provides additional information about the time frame of the action.

Main clause: The main clause is the independent clause that contains a subject and a verb and expresses a complete thought. "When evening came" is not the main clause in the sentence; it is a dependent clause that provides additional information about the time frame of the action in the main clause.

questions.explanation: Adverb clause: An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or another adverb by expressing time, manner, reason, condition, contrast, etc. In this sentence, "when evening came" modifies the verb "rested" by indicating the time of their rest.

questions.text: Read the given passages and answer any SIX questions including Question No. (i) appended to it.Question No. (i) about summary writing is compulsory carrying 08 marks while the rest of the questionscarry 04 marks each. “The second thing that occurs to me is this: One of the biggest curses from which India is suffering — |do not say that other countries are free from it, but, | think our condition is much worse — is bribery and corruption.That really is poison. We must put that down with an iron hand and | hope that you will take adequate measures as soon as it is possible for this Assembly to do so”.....“The next thing that strikes me is this: Here again it is a legacy which has been passed on to us. Along with many other things, good and bad, has arrived great evil, the evil of nepotism and jobbery. | want to make it quite clear that | shall never tolerate any kind of jobbery, nepotism or any influence directly or indirectly brought to bear upon me. Whenever, | will find that such a practice is in vogue or is continuing anywhere, low or high, | shall certainly not countenance it. ;“| know there are people who do not quite agree with the division of India and the partition of the Punjab and Bengal. Much has been said against it, but now that it has been accepted, it is the duty of every one of us to loyally abide by it and honorably act according to the agreement which is now final and binding on all. But you must remember, as | have said, that this mighty revolution that has taken place is unprecedented.Now what shall we do? Now, if we want to make this great State of Pakistan happy and prosperous, we should wholly and solely concentrate on the well-being of the people, and especially of the masses and the poor. If you will work in co-operation, forgetting the past, burying the hatchet, you are bound to succeed. If you change your past and work together in a spirit that every one of you, no matter to what community he belongs, no matter what relations he had with you in the past, no matter what is his colour, caste or creed, is first second and last a citizen of this State with equal rights, privileges, and obligations, there will be no end to the progress you will make.QUESTIONS:(i) Write down summary of the passage and suggest a suitable title.(ii) What is meant by legacy? What legacy is the Quaid referring to?(iii) | What were Quaid-e-Azam’s views regarding nepotism and jobbery?(iv) | What advice did the Quaid give regarding the partition of India (and Pakistan)?(v) What needs to be done in order to make the State (Pakistan) prosperous as advised by the Quaid?( vi) | What is meant by ‘burying the hatchet’ here? Explain with reference to the Quaid’s speech.(vii) Think outside the box and suggest some measures to make Pakistan thrive.(viii) | Write the meanings of any four of the underlined words.
